name: Central great hall layout
description: Medieval great-hall floor-layout strategy in which one dominant rectangular hall occupies 40-50% of the total floor footprint and acts as the building's social, dining and meeting heart, while 2-4 smaller service rooms (kitchen, pantry/store, sleeping chambers, antechamber) are tucked against the long sides and short ends of that hall. The hall is the tallest space in the plan, rising 5 to 8 blocks to expose a timber-truss ceiling and to host a central hearth or long table, whereas the satellite rooms keep ordinary 3-4 block ceilings. This layout is the canonical organisation of medieval manor houses, Viking longhouses, fantasy mead halls and large taverns: the hall is the only space a guest sees first, the side rooms open off it rather than off any corridor.
placement_rules: Place the great hall on the long axis of the footprint so its length is at least 1.3x its width; a square hall reads as a room, not as a hall.
Size the hall between 40% and 50% of the floor area — below that it stops dominating the plan, above that the side rooms become unusable slivers.
Make the hall ceiling 5-8 blocks tall while the satellite rooms stay at 3-4 blocks; the height contrast is what signals 'great hall' from outside via a taller roof ridge.

Place 2 to 4 side rooms against the hall's long walls and short ends; the kitchen takes the end nearest the hearth or chimney, sleeping chambers take the quiet far end, the store/pantry tucks beside the kitchen.
Every side room opens directly onto the hall through a single door — never link two side rooms by an internal corridor, the hall is itself the corridor.
Reserve the geometric centre of the hall for a hearth, long table or fire pit; no door, stair or pillar may land in the central 3x3 block area.
Keep the main entrance on one of the hall's short ends so a visitor walks the full length of the hall before reaching any private room — this is the medieval intimacy gradient.
